questions.prompt: Why offer 10-20% discount on annual vs monthly billing?

choices.text: Cash flow + reduced churn + faster runway. Annual prepaid = cash in hand today vs monthly drips over the year — massive boost to operating capital. Reduced churn: annual customers commit for 12 months; typical SaaS sees 50-80% lower churn on annual vs monthly. Higher-intent customers self-select for annual; they invest more in adoption. The discount is more than recouped by these effects. Most SaaS show monthly as default with 'save 20%' callout for annual to nudge. Net: monthly = customer optionality, terrible cash flow for you; annual = better cash flow + lower churn + higher commitment. Universal pattern; one of the highest-leverage pricing choices for any SaaS.

questions.explanation: Cash upfront + 50-80% lower churn + higher-intent customers. Discount more than pays back.

questions.prompt: Customers complain about paying for dormant per-seat users. Fix?

choices.text: Bill only for ACTIVE users — define active as logged in within N days (typically 30-90). Implement: nightly job flags users active in the period; billing pulls active count; dashboard shows 'active users (billed)' clearly. Dormant users remain in the workspace (can log back in) but don't count until reactivated. Slack pioneered this 'fair billing' pattern; now widespread. Trade-off: slight revenue dip per customer; offset by easier adoption (customers invite everyone), reduced churn (no resentment about wasted seats), and growth as activations rise. Some tools also free view-only roles + paid edit roles — captures value where consumed. Standard fair-billing pattern; addresses the #1 per-seat complaint.

questions.explanation: Bill only active users (logged in within N days). Slack-style fair billing; addresses dormant-user complaint.

questions.prompt: Why include `event_id` on each billable usage event?

choices.text: Idempotency / deduplication. Distributed systems retry on network glitches, queue replays, restart scenarios — the same event may arrive multiple times in the metering pipeline. Without dedup, retries cause double-counting → customer billed twice for one API call → disputes, refunds, credibility damage. With event_id (UUID per event), aggregator checks 'have I processed this ID?' and drops duplicates. Replaying events after a bug fix is safe — duplicates de-dupe. Reliability of metering is a customer-trust issue; errors are remembered. Idempotency keys make billing pipelines tolerant of the chaos distributed systems inevitably produce. Same pattern as API idempotency keys — different layer, same principle: assume retries will happen; design safe under them. Critical for any usage-based pricing where customers scrutinize bills.

questions.explanation: Idempotency for retries/replays. Without it, double-counting; with it, safe replays.

questions.prompt: Card declines: 5-15% on first attempt. Without action, what happens?

choices.text: Customers silently churn. Their service stops; they're confused; they don't know they need to update their card; you lose revenue you would have retained. With proper dunning (structured retry sequence over 2-4 weeks + escalating email + customer-portal link to update card), you recover 50-70% of failed payments. Massive impact: at $50K MRR with 10% failure rate, dunning is worth $25-35K/year in retained revenue. Stripe Smart Retries, Recurly, Chargebee automate this with intelligence (retry on payday, retry after typical resolution windows). Stripe's account-updater also auto-fetches new card numbers from Visa/Mastercard before the customer notices expiry. Combined: involuntary churn drops dramatically. Without dunning: customers churn that you would have kept. ROI on dunning setup is excellent; one of the highest-leverage billing infrastructure investments.

questions.explanation: Silent churn — customers lose service; you lose revenue. Dunning recovers 50-70%. Use Stripe Smart Retries.

choices.text: Anyone knowing your webhook URL can POST fake events without verification — fake cancellations for competitors' customers, fake paid invoices for free service, fake state changes affecting your billing. Stripe signs webhook payloads with HMAC using your webhook secret; verification (`stripe.Webhook.construct_event(payload, sig, secret)`) confirms the request actually came from Stripe. Without verification, your endpoint trusts attacker-controlled input as gospel. Always verify; reject on mismatch with 400. Webhook secret is environment-scoped (test vs live); load from environment variable; never commit. Some teams add additional defense: IP allowlist for Stripe's published IP range as a layer (signature is primary). The 'forgot to verify' bug is a classic supply-chain-like vulnerability where your app accepts attacker-controlled input. Stripe's SDK makes verification one line; no excuse to skip. Same pattern for any webhook — verify the signature is the cheap defense against fake events.

questions.explanation: Attackers can fake events without verification. construct_event() with secret verifies HMAC; reject mismatches.
